name: Compost
description: Aerobic decomposition of kitchen scraps and yard waste into complete soil fertility — feeding soil biology rather than force-feeding plants with synthetic fertilizers.
shelfLife: Finished compost: 1–2 years; improves with aging
effectivenessNote: Superior to synthetic fertilizers for long-term soil health. Improves soil structure, water retention, drainage, and biological diversity simultaneously. Takes 2–6 months but produces complete nutrition.
warnings: Alternate green (nitrogen: food scraps, grass) and brown (carbon: leaves, cardboard) layers
Keep moist but not soggy — like a wrung-out sponge
Turn occasionally to maintain aerobic conditions and prevent odor
Avoid meat, dairy, and oils in outdoor piles (attract pests)
scienceNote: Aerobic bacteria, fungi, and invertebrates break down organic matter into humus — a stable, nutrient-rich material. The carbon:nitrogen ratio (ideally 25–30:1) determines decomposition speed. Compost feeds soil microbiome, which makes nutrients bioavailable to plants through symbiotic relationships.
